nativeRange.habitat: Rocky littoral zone of Lake Malawi; among boulders and rock piles with abundant aufwuchs algae growth; typically in areas with surge and strong water movement
nativeRange.biotope: African Great Lakes — rocky mbuna zone

decorPreferences: large piles of rocks with numerous caves and crevices
sandy substrate (Lake Malawi play sand or aragonite)
open midwater swimming lanes between rock structures
minimal or no live plants (will be uprooted or eaten)
rock structures reaching toward the surface to break sightlines between males

diet.notes: In the wild, the Elongatus Mbuna is primarily an aufwuchs grazer — feeding on the thin mats of algae, diatoms, desmids, and associated microorganisms that coat rocky surfaces in Lake Malawi. In the aquarium, spirulina-based flakes or pellets, algae wafers, and vegetable matter such as blanched spinach or zucchini should make up the bulk of the diet. Small amounts of frozen or live foods such as brine shrimp, daphnia, or mysis are acceptable as an occasional protein supplement, but high-protein foods like bloodworms or beef heart should be strictly limited or avoided altogether. Excess animal protein is associated with Malawi bloat (a potentially fatal digestive condition in cichlids from this lake), and keeping the diet plant-focused is one of the most reliable ways to prevent it. Offering small amounts two to three times daily is generally recommended.

goodWith: other mbuna cichlids of different genera (Labidochromis, Cynotilapia, Iodotropheus)
similarly sized robust African cichlids from different genera
mbuna species with different color patterns to reduce hybrid risk
avoidWith: other Pseudotropheus elongatus color forms (hybridization risk)
peacock cichlids (Aulonocara — typically outcompeted or harassed)
conspecific males (extreme aggression — requires ratio of 1 male to 3+ females minimum)
fish with similar elongated body shape or blue coloration

imageCandidates.notes: Vibrant adult male, 1280x960; sharp, whole fish in frame showing the diagnostic elongated body, electric-blue ground colour with bold black vertical bars and yellow-tipped caudal. Best colour and clarity of the pool.
Likoma Island geographic form, 1000x645; sharp side profile clearly showing dark vertical bars and the yellow egg-spots on the anal fin. Adds a distinct colour-form to the gallery.
Male against rockwork, 700x467; sharp, in-colour blue-and-black barred body with bright orange caudal. Different angle/individual for variety; lower resolution but crisp.

summary: The Elongatus Mbuna is a slender, torpedo-shaped cichlid from the rocky shores of Lake Malawi, known from several distinct geographic color forms including Mbenji Island, Likoma Island, and Mphanga Rocks. Widely considered one of the more aggressive mbuna, it is a maternal mouthbrooder best kept in a large, heavily decorated tank with other robust African cichlids.
careNotes:
Pseudotropheus elongatus is one of the more distinctively shaped mbuna, with a noticeably elongated, torpedo-like body compared to the stockier build typical of the group. It inhabits the rocky littoral zone of Lake Malawi at localities including Mbenji Island, Likoma Island, and Mphanga Rocks, and distinct color forms exist for each population — these should not be mixed in the aquarium to prevent hybridization, which would muddy the gene pool and undermine conservation of distinct geographic variants. Taxonomy is an active area: Pseudotropheus is broadly recognized as paraphyletic (meaning it does not represent a single unified lineage), and P. elongatus has at times been placed in the genus Chindongo alongside other elongated mbuna (e.g., Chindongo elongatus, Chindongo socolofi); checking current literature when acquiring fish from specialist breeders is worthwhile, as the name used can vary.

Aggression is high and directed especially toward conspecifics and fish of similar appearance or coloration. Successful long-term keeping requires a large tank (minimum 300 L, ideally 400+ L) with extensive rockwork that creates numerous caves, breaks sightlines between males, and establishes multiple territories simultaneously. A ratio of at minimum one male to three or four females is strongly advised; a single male with a group of females in a species colony, or a male in a mixed-mbuna community where he is the only representative of the genus, reduces targeted harassment. Overstocking with mbuna (keeping higher numbers to spread aggression) is a widely used strategy in the hobby, but requires larger tanks and excellent filtration. Malawi bloat is a real risk if diet is too protein-rich — keep to algae-based foods as the staple. Water quality must be kept high; Malawi cichlids are sensitive to nitrate accumulation despite their tolerance of hard alkaline conditions. Weekly water changes of 25–30% are recommended. pH must remain consistently alkaline (7.5–8.8); softened or acidic water is harmful and can be fatal.
breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder. Courtship involves the male establishing and defending a spawning site (often a flat rock or cleared sandy patch near rocks), performing vigorous circling and chasing displays toward females, and enticing a ripe female to spawn. Eggs are fertilized using the egg-spot method — the female is attracted to the male's egg-spot markings on the anal fin while releasing eggs, picking up milt in the process. The female then broods the eggs and larvae orally for approximately 21–28 days at typical aquarium temperatures (25°C). A female holding a clutch will not eat and should not be disturbed; mouth-stripping fry early (after day 14–16 if needed) is practiced by experienced breeders. Average clutch size is 15–30 fry. Fry are released as fully formed miniature fish capable of accepting finely crushed spirulina flake and micro-pellets immediately. A species tank or separate brooding tank greatly improves fry survival; in a heavily stocked mbuna community, most fry will be consumed quickly.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Pseudotropheus elongatus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size, temperature range (23–28°C), pH (7.5–8.8), hardness, native range (Lake Malawi rocky zones, multiple localities), diet (aufwuchs/algae), breeding (maternal mouthbrooder, 21–28 day holding), aggression, and taxonomy notes. Taxonomy note: species sometimes listed as Chindongo elongatus in recent literature.

additional.name: FishBase — Pseudotropheus elongatus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 13 cm TL), temperature range, native range (Lake Malawi, Mozambique), taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), diet classification (herbivore/algivore). FishBase lists multiple synonyms and notes the Chindongo elongatus placement in some classification schemes.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — African Cichlid Care Guide
additional.notes: General mbuna/African cichlid husbandry guidance: tank size (minimum 55 gal / ~200 L for smaller mbuna, 75–125 gal for colonies), alkaline hard water requirements, aggression management strategies (overstocking, rocks, male-to-female ratios), Malawi bloat risk with high-protein diets, spirulina-based diet recommendation.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Mbuna Cichlids
additional.notes: Mbuna overview including P. elongatus. Confirmed elongated body form distinguishing it from most mbuna, multiple geographic color forms (Mbenji, Likoma, Mphanga), high aggression, mouthbrooding reproductive strategy, and hybrid avoidance importance. Notes paraphyly of Pseudotropheus and ongoing taxonomic revision.

additional.name: Ad Konings — Lake Malawi Cichlids in their Natural Habitat (via Cichlid-Forum references)
additional.notes: Species profile referencing Konings' field observations. Geographic variant notes (Mbenji Blue Streak, Likoma form, Mphanga form), habitat description (rocky surge zones), feeding ecology (aufwuchs scraping), and depth range (3–20 m). Confirms male-only coloration difference and female drab/barred pattern.

fish.breedingNotes: The Elongatus Mbuna is a maternal mouthbrooder: after spawning, the female holds the fertilized eggs and developing larvae inside her mouth for approximately 21–28 days. Clutches typically run 15–30 fry, which are released as fully formed juveniles capable of feeding immediately on micro-crushed spirulina flake. A separate brooding tank or species-only setup significantly improves fry survival, as in a heavily stocked community most released fry will be consumed quickly. Males are widely considered highly aggressive during spawning and can injure or kill females; keeping multiple females and providing dense rockwork are essential to distributing that aggression.
